teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes that all sincere spiritual practices, when purified, converge on the same inner realization.

teachings.teaching: The material world is a manifestation of Kṛṣṇa's energies; everything is pervaded by Him.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga view that the entire cosmos is a projection of the Supreme Consciousness, and yoga aims to realize this unity.

teachings.teaching: The jīva is deluded by māyā, identifying with the body and possessions, and suffers in saṃsāra.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga teaches that ignorance of one's true nature is the root cause of bondage, and the practice of yoga dispels this ignorance.

teachings.teaching: Surrender to Kṛṣṇa is the ultimate refuge; by His grace, one can attain liberation.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, surrender (prapatti) is the culmination of the path, where the individual self merges in the Supreme Self through grace.

chapter_summary: Akrūra, after witnessing Kṛṣṇa's divine form in the Yamunā, returns to Mathurā with Kṛṣṇa and Balarāma. They enter the city, are welcomed by the citizens, and Kṛṣṇa kills a washerman who refuses to give garments, then blesses a garland-maker who offers him a garland.

story.title: Entry into Mathurā and Encounters
story.arc: Kṛṣṇa's pastimes in Mathurā
story.timeline_note: After the Yamunā episode, before the wrestling match with Kaṃsa's wrestlers.
story.comic_potential: The washerman's beheading and the garland-maker's devotion provide contrasting humorous and heartwarming moments.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's grace is impartial; He blesses both those who oppose and those who serve, according to their attitude.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the principle of karma-yoga: actions performed with ego lead to bondage, while those offered to the Divine lead to liberation. The washerman's ego leads to death, while Sudāmā's devotion brings blessings.

teachings.teaching: True purity comes from devotion, not external rituals.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that inner purification through bhakti is superior to external cleansing. Akrūra's vision of Kṛṣṇa in the water symbolizes the inner realization that transcends ritual bathing.

chapter_summary: This chapter describes the aftermath of Krishna's granting a boon to the deity of the Syamantaka jewel, Sudarsana, and his departure with Balarama. The boon includes friendship with devotees, compassion for all beings, and prosperity for his lineage.

story.title: Krishna's Boon to Sudarsana
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: After the Syamantaka jewel episode
story.comic_potential: The boon granting scene could be depicted with divine radiance and blessings.

teachings.teaching: Cultivate friendship with devotees and compassion for all beings.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the cultivation of bhakti (devotion) and karuna (compassion) as essential practices for spiritual progress.

chapter_summary: Krishna and Balarama enter Mathura, encounter the hunchbacked woman Trivakra who offers them sandalwood paste, and Krishna straightens her back. They then break the great bow in the arena, kill Kamsa's guards, and spend the night in the city. Kamsa, terrified by omens, organizes a wrestling match the next day, inviting Nanda and the cowherds.

story.title: Krishna and Balarama in Mathura: The Breaking of the Bow and the Wrestling Match
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Mathura leading to the slaying of Kamsa
story.timeline_note: This chapter occurs after Krishna and Balarama have arrived in Mathura for the first time, before the wrestling match and Kamsa's death.
story.comic_potential: High: The encounter with Trivakra is humorous and tender; the breaking of the bow is dramatic; the omens seen by Kamsa are visually striking.

teachings.teaching: Krishna's touch straightens the crooked, symbolizing spiritual transformation.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this represents the straightening of the kundalini and the purification of the nadis through divine contact.

teachings.teaching: The breaking of the bow signifies the destruction of ego and material pride.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga interprets the bow as the mind's tension; breaking it is the dissolution of mental constructs through pranayama and meditation.

teachings.teaching: Kamsa's omens reflect the inner turmoil of a soul approaching death.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, these omens are seen as signs of the dissolution of the ego (ahamkara) when one is about to realize the Self.

chapter_summary: Krishna and Balarama enter the wrestling arena after killing the elephant Kuvalayapida. The people praise them, recalling their divine feats. The wrestler Canura challenges them to a match, and Krishna accepts, emphasizing fair play.

story.title: Krishna and Balarama Enter the Arena
story.arc: Kamsa's plot to kill Krishna
story.timeline_note: After killing the elephant, Krishna and Balarama enter the wrestling arena.
story.comic_potential: High: dramatic elephant fight, crowd reactions, and wrestling challenge.

teachings.teaching: One should act to please the king and thus gain prosperity; opposing the king brings ruin.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the principle of aligning one's actions with the cosmic order (dharma) to achieve harmony and success.

teachings.teaching: Fair play in combat: opponents should be evenly matched to avoid adharma.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes balanced effort and righteous action; this teaching underscores the importance of fairness in all endeavors.

chapter_summary: Krishna and Balarama defeat and kill the wrestlers Canura, Mustika, Kuta, Sala, and Tosala in the Mathura arena, then Krishna kills Kamsa. The women of Kamsa's family lament, and Krishna and Balarama release their parents Devaki and Vasudeva from bondage.

story.title: Krishna and Balarama Kill the Wrestlers and Kamsa
story.arc: Kamsa's death and liberation of Devaki and Vasudeva
story.timeline_note: This chapter occurs after Krishna and Balarama have entered the Mathura arena and before the subsequent events of Kamsa's funeral and the installation of Ugrasena as king.
story.comic_potential: High: The wrestling match with Canura and Mustika, the dramatic leap onto the platform, and the killing of Kamsa are visually striking and action-packed.

teachings.teaching: The Lord protects the righteous and destroys the wicked; even in a seemingly unfair fight, divine justice prevails.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this represents the inner battle where the practitioner must overcome the wrestlers (sensory distractions) with the help of the divine will (Krishna) and strength (Balarama).

teachings.teaching: One should not associate with a place where adharma (unrighteousness) is allowed to flourish; the wise avoid such assemblies.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that the mind must avoid environments that foster negative tendencies; the arena of Kamsa symbolizes the mind filled with ego and desire.

teachings.teaching: The gopis' devotion is the highest; they see the Lord in every activity and are absorbed in love for Him.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga teaches that constant remembrance of the divine (like the gopis' singing) is a form of kriya-yoga, purifying the heart.

teachings.teaching: Even a tyrant like Kamsa, who constantly thought of Krishna, attained liberation because his mind was fixed on the Lord.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma notes that even negative concentration on the divine can lead to liberation, but the ideal is loving devotion.

chapter_summary: Krishna and Balarama return to Mathura, reunite with their parents Vasudeva and Devaki, and console them. They install Ugrasena as king, welcome back the Yadava clans, and send Nanda and the gopas back to Vrindavan. They then undergo sacred thread ceremony with Garga, study under Sandipani in Avanti, and after mastering all arts, retrieve Sandipani's son from the ocean and Yama's city, demonstrating their divine nature.

story.title: Krishna and Balarama's Return to Mathura and Education
story.arc: Krishna's early life and education
story.timeline_note: After Kamsa's death, before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: Krishna and Balarama as divine students mastering everything instantly, then retrieving the guru's son from the ocean and Yama's city.

teachings.teaching: One must serve parents and elders; failing to do so leads to suffering after death.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the importance of selfless service (seva) as a foundational practice for purifying the mind and preparing for higher yoga.

teachings.teaching: A guru's debt is repaid by fulfilling his wish, even if it requires extraordinary effort.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga views guru-seva as a direct means to accelerate spiritual progress, as the guru is a conduit for divine grace.

teachings.teaching: The divine descends as a human to teach dharma through lila (play).
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga interprets Krishna's human-like actions as a demonstration that even the divine follows the path of duty and devotion, inspiring practitioners to do the same.

chapter_summary: Uddhava is sent by Krishna to Vrindavan to console Nanda, Yashoda, and the gopis who are suffering from separation. Uddhava delivers Krishna's message, describing his divine nature and assuring them of his return. The chapter ends with the gopis' morning activities and their curiosity about Uddhava's arrival.

story.title: Uddhava's Visit to Vrindavan
story.arc: Krishna's separation and Uddhava's consolation
story.timeline_note: After Krishna's departure to Mathura, before his return to Vrindavan.
story.comic_potential: The gopis' morning activities and their curiosity about Uddhava's chariot could be depicted humorously.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the inner self of all beings, and one should see Him everywhere.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the realization of the Self as the witness, leading to equanimity.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is beyond material qualities and acts only for the protection of the righteous.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that the divine play (lila) is a teaching tool to inspire devotion and detachment.

teachings.teaching: Devotion to Krishna is the highest path, surpassing all worldly duties.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Kriya-Yoga's focus on inner devotion and surrender to the divine will.

chapter_summary: Uddhava visits the gopis of Vrindavan as a messenger from Krishna. The gopis express their intense separation and love for Krishna, questioning his faithfulness. Uddhava delivers Krishna's message of philosophical consolation, emphasizing the eternal nature of the soul and the importance of constant remembrance. The gopis, though comforted, remain deeply attached to Krishna.

story.title: Uddhava's Visit to the Gopis
story.arc: Krishna's separation and the gopis' devotion
story.timeline_note: After Krishna's departure to Mathura and the killing of Kamsa, Uddhava is sent to console the gopis.
story.comic_potential: The gopis' dramatic lamentations and their playful yet poignant accusations against Krishna could be depicted with expressive dialogues and emotional scenes.

teachings.teaching: The soul is distinct from the body and the material world; it is pure consciousness and unaffected by the three states of waking, dreaming, and deep sleep.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this aligns with the practice of witnessing the mind's modifications and realizing the self as pure awareness beyond the gunas.

teachings.teaching: Constant remembrance of Krishna (or the divine) leads to liberation and union with the divine.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes continuous mindfulness (smriti) as a key Kriya-Yoga technique to transcend the ego and realize oneness.

teachings.teaching: Detachment from worldly relationships and possessions is necessary for spiritual progress.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga advocates inner renunciation (tyaga) through disciplined practice, not mere physical abandonment.

teachings.teaching: The highest happiness is in freedom from desire (nairashya), as exemplified by the prostitute Pingala.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma interprets this as the bliss of inner contentment achieved through Kriya-Yoga, where one rests in the self.

chapter_summary: Uddhava visits Vrindavan to console the gopis and Nanda's family after Krishna's departure. He stays for several months, singing Krishna's pastimes and alleviating their grief. The gopis' intense love for Krishna is praised, and Uddhava returns to Mathura, reporting their devotion to Krishna.

story.title: Uddhava's Consolation of the Gopis
story.arc: Krishna's departure from Vrindavan and the aftermath
story.timeline_note: After Krishna leaves Vrindavan for Mathura, Uddhava is sent to console the residents.
story.comic_potential: The gopis' intense separation and Uddhava's philosophical praise create a poignant contrast.

teachings.teaching: The gopis' love for Krishna is the highest form of devotion, surpassing even the desires of great sages and yogis.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the pinnacle of bhakti where the practitioner's entire being is absorbed in the divine, transcending all worldly attachments.

teachings.teaching: Even those who are seemingly unqualified (like the gopis) can attain the highest spiritual perfection through pure devotion.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that Kriya-Yoga is accessible to all, regardless of background, as devotion (bhakti) is the key.

teachings.teaching: The dust of the feet of the gopis is supremely purifying, and their songs about Krishna purify the three worlds.
teachings.lens_note: This highlights the power of association with pure devotees, a core principle in Kriya-Yoga for spiritual progress.

teachings.teaching: The residents of Vrindavan pray that their mind, speech, and body be always engaged in Krishna's service.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga teaches the integration of all faculties in devotion, aligning with this prayer for holistic surrender.

chapter_summary: Krishna visits the courtesan's house to fulfill her desire, then goes to Akrura's house. Akrura praises Krishna as the Supreme Lord and requests liberation from material bondage. Krishna sends Akrura to Hastinapura to check on the Pandavas.

story.title: Krishna's Visit to the Courtesan and Akrura
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: After the killing of Kamsa, before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: The contrast between Krishna's divine nature and his mundane activities like visiting a courtesan and sending a messenger.

teachings.teaching: The Supreme Lord is not bound by material qualities or actions; He is pure consciousness.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the witness consciousness (sakshi) that remains unaffected by prakriti.

teachings.teaching: The Lord incarnates to protect dharma and relieve the earth's burden.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga sees this as the descent of divine energy to restore balance in the practitioner's life.

teachings.teaching: Saints purify by their mere sight, unlike holy waters or deities made of material elements.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, the guru's presence is the ultimate purifier, more effective than external rituals.

teachings.teaching: One should seek the shelter of the Lord, who is dear to His devotees and fulfills all desires.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes surrender (prapatti) as the key to Kriya-Yoga practice.

chapter_summary: Akrūra visits Hastinapura, meets Dhṛtarāṣṭra, Bhīṣma, Vidura, Kuntī, and others. Kuntī laments her sons' plight and prays to Kṛṣṇa. Akrūra advises Dhṛtarāṣṭra to treat the Pāṇḍavas equally, warning against adharma. Dhṛtarāṣṭra acknowledges his attachment to his sons and the divine plan. Akrūra returns to Dvārakā and reports to Rāma and Kṛṣṇa.

story.title: Akrūra's Mission to Hastinapura
story.arc: Pre-Kurukshetra diplomacy and Kuntī's lament
story.timeline_note: After the Pāṇḍavas' exile, before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: Akrūra's diplomatic visit, Kuntī's emotional outburst, Dhṛtarāṣṭra's inner conflict.

teachings.teaching: One is born alone, dies alone, and experiences the results of one's actions alone.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the solitary nature of spiritual practice; the yogi must rely on inner effort, not external associations.

teachings.teaching: Wealth acquired through adharma is taken by others; the unwise person who accumulates it is abandoned by his possessions and falls into darkness.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's commentary: Adharmic actions create karmic debts that bind the soul; true wealth is spiritual merit earned through disciplined practice.

teachings.teaching: This world is like a dream, illusion, and mental construct; one should control the mind and remain equal and peaceful.
teachings.lens_note: Kriya-Yoga teaches that the world is a projection of the mind; through meditation and pranayama, one transcends maya and attains inner peace.

teachings.teaching: No one can change the will of Īśvara, who descends to relieve the earth's burden.
teachings.lens_note: The divine plan is beyond human comprehension; surrender to the will of God is the essence of bhakti-yoga.

chapter_summary: Jarāsandha, king of Magadha, attacks Mathurā with a vast army to avenge his son-in-law Kaṃsa's death. Kṛṣṇa and Balarāma, understanding their divine purpose to relieve the earth's burden, engage in battle. Kṛṣṇa decimates the enemy forces, while Balarāma captures Jarāsandha but spares him at Kṛṣṇa's request. Jarāsandha returns to Magadha, later attacking seventeen more times, each time defeated. On the eighteenth, the Yavana king Kālayavana, sent by Nārada, besieges Mathurā. Kṛṣṇa builds a fortress city in the sea, Dvārakā, to protect the Yadus.

story.title: Jarāsandha's Attack and the Building of Dvārakā
story.arc: Kṛṣṇa's early life and battles
story.timeline_note: After Kaṃsa's death, Jarāsandha attacks Mathurā multiple times; this chapter covers the first and eighteenth attacks.
story.comic_potential: High: epic battle scenes, divine weapons, miraculous city construction.

teachings.teaching: Kṛṣṇa incarnates to relieve the earth's burden and protect the righteous.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the principle of dharma-rakṣā: the divine descends to restore cosmic balance when adharma prevails.

teachings.teaching: A true warrior does not boast but demonstrates prowess through action.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes kriya (action) over vikalpa (vacillation); Kṛṣṇa's response to Jarāsandha's taunt exemplifies decisive action without ego.

teachings.teaching: Sparing an enemy can be strategic, not a sign of weakness.
teachings.lens_note: Kṛṣṇa's mercy on Jarāsandha shows that compassion must be balanced with long-term vision, a key tenet in Kriya-Yoga's approach to karma.

chapter_summary: Krishna builds Dvaraka city with divine gifts from the gods, including the assembly hall Sudharma and the Parijata tree. He then goes out unarmed to meet the approaching enemy.

story.title: Building Dvaraka and the Divine Gifts
story.arc: Krishna establishes his capital and prepares for battle
story.timeline_note: After the construction of Dvaraka, before the battle with Jarasandha's army.
story.comic_potential: Krishna walking out unarmed with a garland, surprising the enemy.

teachings.teaching: The divine gifts (Sudharma, Parijata, horses, treasures) are given by the gods to Krishna as part of his divine plan.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, these gifts symbolize the inner powers (siddhis) that arise when one is established in the Self. The assembly hall Sudharma represents the state of pure consciousness where one is free from mortal limitations.

teachings.teaching: Krishna goes out unarmed, showing that true strength lies in divine will, not weapons.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes that the yogi, established in the Self, acts without personal motive and is protected by the divine. Krishna's unarmed approach symbolizes the power of non-attachment and surrender.

chapter_summary: Mucukunda, a great king of the Ikshvaku dynasty, had been guarding the gods for a long time. After being granted a boon, he chose to sleep in a cave. The Yavana king, pursuing Krishna, entered the cave and kicked Mucukunda, who then burned him to ashes with his angry glance. Krishna then revealed himself to Mucukunda, who recognized him as Narayana and offered prayers, seeking liberation from material entanglement.

story.title: The Deliverance of Mucukunda
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Mathura and Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: After Krishna killed Kamsa and before the establishment of Dvaraka.
story.comic_potential: Mucukunda's long sleep and the Yavana's mistaken kick provide humorous contrast to the serious spiritual teachings.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's names, births, and activities are infinite and cannot be fully enumerated.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the limitless nature of divine grace and the futility of intellectual grasping; one should engage in devotional practice rather than counting.

teachings.teaching: Material life is like a blind well; one who does not worship the Lord is like an animal fallen into it.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as a call to Kriya-Yoga practice to awaken from the stupor of sense gratification and realize the self.

teachings.teaching: Time, as the Lord's agent, devours even the proud who are attached to family and kingdom.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Sharma's emphasis on the urgency of spiritual practice before death; Kriya-Yoga prepares one to face time fearlessly.

chapter_summary: In this chapter, King Mucukunda recounts his past lives and the futility of worldly pleasures, then seeks refuge in Krishna. Krishna grants him unwavering devotion and promises liberation in his next life as a brahmana.

story.title: Mucukunda's Liberation
story.arc: Mucukunda, after waking from a long sleep, realizes the transience of worldly power and seeks Krishna's shelter. Krishna blesses him with unwavering devotion and promises liberation in his next birth.
story.timeline_note: After the battle with Kālayavana, Mucukunda meets Krishna.
story.comic_potential: Mucukunda's dramatic realization of life's futility and his heartfelt plea for liberation, followed by Krishna's compassionate response, offers a poignant and uplifting scene.

teachings.teaching: Worldly pleasures and power are temporary and lead to suffering; true happiness lies in devotion to God.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this teaching emphasizes the need to turn inward and detach from sensory objects, aligning with the practice of pratyāhāra.

teachings.teaching: Association with saints (sat-saṅga) leads to devotion and liberation.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga stresses the importance of satsaṅga for purifying the mind and awakening bhakti.

teachings.teaching: Even a king should seek nothing but the lotus feet of the Lord, which is the highest boon.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the Kriya-Yoga ideal of single-pointed devotion (ekānta-bhakti) as the ultimate goal.

teachings.teaching: Devotion to Krishna destroys all sins, even for those engaged in worldly duties like hunting.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga teaches that karma-yoga performed with devotion purifies the heart.

chapter_summary: This chapter recounts events after Krishna's liberation of Nanda and the gopas from the demon. It describes the departure of the liberated king (Nanda) to Badarikashrama for penance, Krishna's return to Dvaraka, the attack by Jarasandha, the flight of Krishna and Balarama to Mount Pravarṣaṇa, Jarasandha's attempt to burn them, their escape, and the marriage of Krishna to Rukmiṇī, including the exchange of messages via a brahmana messenger.

story.title: The Liberation of Nanda and the Marriage of Rukmiṇī
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Dvaraka and his divine marriages
story.timeline_note: After the liberation of Nanda from the demon, Krishna returns to Dvaraka, then faces Jarasandha's attack, escapes, and later marries Rukmiṇī.
story.comic_potential: The dramatic escape from Jarasandha's fire on the mountain and the secret message exchange for the svayamvara have high comic potential.

teachings.teaching: Contentment is the greatest wealth; even a poor contented man sleeps happily, while a discontented king suffers.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, contentment (santoṣa) is a key niyama that purifies the mind and allows one to abide in the self, free from external fluctuations.

teachings.teaching: The wise bow to brahmanas who are content with their lot, compassionate, egoless, and peaceful.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the Kriya-Yoga emphasis on humility and service to realized beings as a means to accelerate spiritual progress.

teachings.teaching: A king who protects his subjects and ensures their happiness is dear to the Lord.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework sees righteous action (dharma) in the world as an expression of inner yoga, where leadership is a form of service.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's qualities and form captivate the senses and remove the distress of the heart.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, meditation on the divine form (rūpa) and qualities (guṇa) is a powerful technique to still the mind and attain samadhi.

teachings.teaching: Devotees yearn for the dust of the Lord's lotus feet to cleanse their ignorance, just as Śiva does.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga practice of surrendering the ego and seeking grace through devotion (bhakti) as a path to liberation.

chapter_summary: Krishna, having received Rukmini's message, decides to abduct her before her marriage to Shishupala. He travels to Kundina with a brahmana messenger. Meanwhile, Rukmini's father prepares for the wedding, and many enemy kings assemble. Rukmini anxiously awaits Krishna, and the brahmana returns to reassure her. Krishna and Balarama arrive and are honored by the king. Rukmini goes to the temple of Ambika (Parvati) to pray, then leaves the temple, ready for Krishna to take her.

story.title: The Abduction of Rukmini
story.arc: Krishna's marriages and pastimes
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Krishna's return from Dvaraka and before the great war at Kurukshetra.
story.comic_potential: High: romantic tension, suspense of the abduction plan, assembly of rival kings, and Rukmini's prayer scene.

teachings.teaching: Krishna reciprocates the love of his devotee; he is equally eager to meet Rukmini.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the principle of 'sadhaka-bhagavan samvada' – the dialogue between the practitioner and the divine, where the divine responds to sincere longing.

teachings.teaching: The divine plan overcomes obstacles; Krishna will forcibly take Rukmini despite opposition.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes that obstacles in spiritual practice are overcome by the grace of the guru and the power of kriya, just as Krishna overcomes the kings.

teachings.teaching: Rukmini's anxiety and prayer demonstrate complete surrender to Krishna.
teachings.lens_note: This exemplifies 'prapatti' (self-surrender), a key aspect of Kriya-Yoga where the practitioner relies entirely on the divine will.

teachings.teaching: The townspeople's joy at Krishna's arrival shows the natural attraction of the soul to the divine.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the spontaneous joy (ananda) that arises when the practitioner connects with the inner self through kriya.

chapter_summary: Rukmiṇī, adorned with exquisite beauty, walks slowly to the temple, captivating the kings who become infatuated. She glances at them shyly but sees Kṛṣṇa. As she prepares to board his chariot, Kṛṣṇa abducts her in the presence of the assembled kings, who are humiliated and enraged, especially Jarāsandha and others.

story.title: The Abduction of Rukmiṇī
story.arc: Kṛṣṇa's marriage to Rukmiṇī
story.timeline_note: After Rukmiṇī's svayamvara announcement, Kṛṣṇa abducts her before the ceremony.
story.comic_potential: High drama: Rukmiṇī's slow walk, the kings' infatuation, and the sudden abduction.

teachings.teaching: The divine beauty of the Lord's consort can captivate even the most powerful kings.
teachings.lens_note: Illustrates the power of divine feminine beauty.

teachings.teaching: The Lord protects his devotees by removing obstacles, even through seemingly unconventional means.
teachings.lens_note: Kṛṣṇa's abduction is a form of protection for Rukmiṇī.

chapter_summary: After Krishna abducts Rukmini, the Yadava army defeats the pursuing kings led by Jarasandha. Rukmi, Rukmini's brother, challenges Krishna but is defeated and humiliated. Balarama rebukes Krishna for disfiguring Rukmi and teaches Rukmini about the nature of the self and detachment.

story.title: Krishna's Marriage and the Defeat of Rukmi
story.arc: Krishna abducts Rukmini, defeats the assembled kings, and spares Rukmi after disfiguring him; Balarama teaches Rukmini about the self.
story.timeline_note: Immediately after Krishna's abduction of Rukmini from her svayamvara.
story.comic_potential: Rukmi's boastful challenge and subsequent humiliation, including having his head shaved, has comedic elements.

teachings.teaching: The self (atman) is distinct from the body; attachment to the body causes suffering.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this aligns with the practice of disidentification from the body-mind complex through meditation and self-inquiry.

teachings.teaching: One should not grieve over the inevitable; the world is under the control of time and destiny.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes acceptance of divine will and karma, reducing emotional turmoil.

teachings.teaching: True knowledge removes ignorance-born sorrow.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this is the goal of jnana yoga: realizing the self as pure consciousness.

chapter_summary: After Rukmiṇī's abduction, Rukmin, enraged, builds a new city named Bhojakaṭa and vows not to enter Kuṇḍina until he kills Kṛṣṇa. Meanwhile, Kṛṣṇa marries Rukmiṇī in Dvārakā with great celebration, and the news astonishes neighboring kings.

story.title: Rukmin's Vow and the Marriage of Kṛṣṇa and Rukmiṇī
story.arc: After the abduction, Rukmin's anger leads him to build a new city and vow revenge, while Kṛṣṇa marries Rukmiṇī in Dvārakā amid great festivities.
story.timeline_note: Immediately following Rukmiṇī's abduction from Kuṇḍina.
story.comic_potential: Rukmin's dramatic vow and the lavish wedding celebration provide contrasting scenes of rage and joy.

teachings.teaching: Let go of mental agitation and steady the mind with intelligence (vaimanasyaṃ parityajya mano buddhyā samādadhe).
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this is a direct instruction for pratyahara (withdrawal of senses) and dharana (concentration), where one consciously releases mental disturbances and fixes the mind on a higher object.

teachings.teaching: One should not enter a place until a goal is accomplished (ahatvā durmatiṃ kṛṣṇam apratyūhya yavīyasīm kuṇḍinaṃ na pravekṣyāmīty uktvā).
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as a metaphor for the yogic vow (sankalpa) to not return to mundane consciousness until the divine is realized.

teachings.teaching: The Lord marries His devotee in due form (bhagavān bhīṣmaka-sutām evaṃ nirjitya bhūmi-pān puram ānīya vidhi-vad upayeme).
teachings.lens_note: This symbolizes the union of the individual soul (jiva) with the Supreme (Krishna) through proper spiritual practice (vidhi-vat), as in Kriya-Yoga's systematic sadhana.

teachings.teaching: Seeing the Lord with His consort brings great joy (rukmiṇyā ramayopetaṃ dṛṣṭvā kṛṣṇaṃ śriyaḥ patim).
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see this as the bliss of samadhi, where the practitioner experiences the divine couple (Radha-Krishna) as the ultimate reality.

chapter_summary: This chapter narrates the story of Pradyumna, the son of Krishna and Rukmini, who is an incarnation of Kamadeva. Kidnapped as an infant by the demon Shambara, he is swallowed by a fish, recovered, and raised by Mayavati (Rati in disguise). Upon learning his identity, he defeats and kills Shambara, marries Rati, and returns to Dvaraka, bringing joy to his family.

story.title: The Return of Pradyumna
story.arc: Pradyumna's abduction, growth, and victorious return to Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Krishna's marriage to Rukmini and before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: High; includes a baby swallowed by a fish, a demon's kitchen, a hidden identity, and a dramatic battle with magical weapons.

teachings.teaching: The divine play (lila) of the Lord includes apparent loss and reunion, demonstrating that nothing is truly lost in the spiritual realm.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the principle of 'pratyahara' (withdrawal of senses) and 'dharana' (concentration): the soul (Pradyumna) is temporarily obscured by maya (Shambara) but eventually re-emerges through divine grace and inner knowledge (Narada's guidance).

teachings.teaching: The power of divine knowledge (vidya) overcomes all illusions (maya).
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that the 'Maha-maya' vidya given to Pradyumna represents the Kriya-Yoga technique that destroys all delusions, enabling the practitioner to conquer the demon of ignorance.

teachings.teaching: True identity is spiritual, not material; recognition of one's divine nature brings liberation.
teachings.lens_note: Pradyumna's realization of his identity as Kamadeva and son of Krishna mirrors the Kriya-Yoga goal of self-realization (atma-jnana) through meditation and guru's grace.

chapter_summary: This chapter narrates the story of the Syamantaka jewel. Satrajit, a devotee of the Sun god, receives the jewel from Surya. His brother Prasena is killed by a lion while wearing it, and the lion is killed by Jambavan. Krishna is falsely accused of taking the jewel, so he tracks down the truth, fights Jambavan for 28 days, and recovers the jewel. He returns it to Satrajit, who then gives his daughter Satyabhama in marriage to Krishna to clear his name.

story.title: The Syamantaka Jewel
story.arc: Krishna's divine play to clear his name and acquire the jewel and Satyabhama
story.timeline_note: Occurs during Krishna's residence in Dvaraka, after the killing of Kamsa.
story.comic_potential: The mistaken identity of Satrajit as Surya, the children's confusion, the 28-day fight with Jambavan, and the dramatic return from the cave.

teachings.teaching: False accusations can be cleared by righteous action and truth.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the purification of karma through selfless action (nishkama karma). Krishna's journey to clear his name is an example of acting without attachment to outcome, yet upholding truth.

teachings.teaching: The divine is beyond material perception; even the Sun god is a devotee of Krishna.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's lens emphasizes that all deities are manifestations of the one Supreme. The Sun's worship of Krishna shows the unity of all paths.

teachings.teaching: Material wealth (the jewel) can cause conflict, but when offered to the divine, it brings peace.
teachings.lens_note: The Syamantaka jewel represents material opulence. Its proper use is in service of the divine, as Satrajit finally does by giving it to Krishna. This aligns with Kriya-Yoga's teaching of offering the fruits of action to God.

teachings.teaching: Repentance and surrender lead to forgiveness and grace.
teachings.lens_note: Satrajit's remorse and offering of his daughter and jewel symbolize the soul's surrender to the divine, which is a key step in Kriya-Yoga.

chapter_summary: Krishna and Balarama visit Hastinapura to console the Pandavas and Kunti after the fire incident. Meanwhile, Satrajit is murdered by Shatadhanva for the Syamantaka jewel. Shatadhanva flees, pursued by Krishna and Balarama. Krishna kills Shatadhanva but the jewel is not found. Balarama goes to Mithila, while Krishna returns to Dwaraka. Later, Krishna retrieves the jewel from Akrura, who had hidden it, and clears his name.

story.title: The Syamantaka Jewel
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Dwaraka
story.timeline_note: After the Pandavas' escape from the lac house, before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: High; chase scene, mistaken identity, jewel theft, and divine intervention.

teachings.teaching: Even the Lord's devotees may face false accusations, but truth prevails.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the purification of karma through surrender; Krishna's acceptance of blame and subsequent vindication mirrors the yogi's journey through cleansing.

teachings.teaching: Material wealth (the jewel) can cause conflict and death, but when offered to the Lord, it brings peace.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework emphasizes detachment from objects of desire; the Syamantaka jewel symbolizes the mind's attachment to sense objects, which must be surrendered to the divine.

teachings.teaching: The Lord protects His devotees and removes obstacles.
teachings.lens_note: Krishna's pursuit of Shatadhanva and recovery of the jewel demonstrates the guru's role in removing spiritual obstacles for the sincere practitioner.

chapter_summary: Krishna visits the Pandavas in Indraprastha, marries Kalindi (Yamuna's daughter), Mitravinda, and Satya (Nagnajit's daughter) after subduing seven bulls, and recounts the burning of Khandava forest and the gifts from Agni and Maya.

story.title: Krishna's Marriages and the Khandava Forest Episode
story.arc: Krishna's visit to Indraprastha and his marriages to Kalindi, Mitravinda, and Satya, with flashback to the burning of Khandava forest.
story.timeline_note: Events occur after the Pandavas' establishment in Indraprastha, before the Rajasuya sacrifice.
story.comic_potential: Krishna's playful subduing of seven bulls and the humorous confusion of Duryodhana in Maya's assembly hall.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's presence removes all sins (anga-sanga-hatainasaḥ).
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this signifies that contact with the divine through practice purifies the subtle body.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the inner witness who removes afflictions of those who remember Him (smaratāṃ śaśvat kleśān haṃsi hṛdi sthitaḥ).
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that constant remembrance (smarana) in Kriya-Yoga dissolves karmic impressions.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is self-sufficient and acts out of love, not need (ātmānandena pūrṇasya).
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the Kriya-Yoga principle that the divine is complete and actions are for the upliftment of others.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's pastimes are effortless and playful (līlayā).
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's view, this illustrates that divine actions are spontaneous and without strain, a model for the practitioner's effortless effort.

chapter_summary: Krishna marries many wives, including Satya (daughter of King Nagnajit of Kosala), Bhadra (daughter of Srutakirti), Lakshmana (daughter of the Madra king), and thousands of others rescued from the demon Bhauma. Arjuna assists in the marriage of Satya by defeating rival kings.

story.title: Krishna's Marriages
story.arc: Krishna marries multiple wives, including Satya, Bhadra, and Lakshmana, and rescues thousands of women from Bhauma.
story.timeline_note: After the defeat of Bhauma, Krishna marries the rescued women.
story.comic_potential: Arjuna's lion-like routing of rival kings could be depicted humorously.

teachings.teaching: Krishna's marriages demonstrate his divine pastimes and his role as the supreme enjoyer.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the principle of divine play (lila) and the integration of all aspects of life into spiritual practice.

teachings.teaching: Arjuna's assistance shows the importance of service to the divine.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes selfless action (karma yoga) as a means to purify the mind and attain union with the divine.

chapter_summary: King Parīkṣit asks Śuka about how Lord Kṛṣṇa killed the demon Bhauma (Naraka) and rescued the captive princesses. Śuka narrates: Indra informed Kṛṣṇa of Bhauma's atrocities. Kṛṣṇa, with Satyabhāmā, rode Garuḍa to Prāgjyotiṣa, broke through its defenses, and killed the five-headed Mura and Bhauma's seven sons. Bhauma attacked with an elephant army but was beheaded by Kṛṣṇa's disc. Earth goddess (Bhūmi) offered Bhauma's earrings and praised Kṛṣṇa, asking mercy for her son. Kṛṣṇa granted it, entered Bhauma's palace, found 16,000 captive princesses, and sent them to Dvārakā. He returned Indra's earrings, uprooted the pārijāta tree for Satyabhāmā, and later married all the princesses simultaneously by expanding into many forms.

story.title: The Killing of Naraka and the Rescue of the Princesses
story.arc: Kṛṣṇa's pastimes as the supreme lord protecting the world and fulfilling his devotees' desires.
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Kṛṣṇa's marriage to Satyabhāmā and before the Syamantaka episode.
story.comic_potential: High: dramatic battle scenes, divine weapons, miraculous rescues, and a mass wedding.

teachings.teaching: The Lord protects the righteous and punishes the wicked, as seen in Kṛṣṇa's destruction of Bhauma and his forces.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this represents the inner purification where the divine will (Kṛṣṇa) destroys the ego's attachments (Bhauma) and its defenses (the demons).

teachings.teaching: Devotion and surrender to the Lord bring liberation, as Bhūmi's prayer and offering of earrings show.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that bhakti (devotion) is the highest yoga; here, Bhūmi's humble prayer exemplifies the attitude of surrender that dissolves karma.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the ultimate refuge for all beings, as the captive princesses mentally accept Kṛṣṇa as their husband.
teachings.lens_note: This illustrates the Kriya-Yoga principle of fixing the mind on the divine, leading to union with the supreme.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's pastimes are beyond material comprehension, as he simultaneously married 16,000 wives in separate forms.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma views this as a metaphor for the soul's ability to experience divine bliss in all aspects of life when established in yoga.

chapter_summary: Krishna playfully tests Rukmini's devotion by pretending to be unworthy of her, but she responds with a profound discourse on his supreme nature and her unwavering love. Krishna then reassures her, praising her devotion and explaining the true purpose of marriage.

story.title: Krishna's Playful Test of Rukmini's Devotion
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: After Krishna's marriage to Rukmini, early in their domestic life.
story.comic_potential: High. Krishna's teasing and Rukmini's dramatic reaction, followed by her eloquent rebuttal, create a rich comedic and romantic dynamic.

teachings.teaching: True devotion is unwavering even when the Lord appears to reject the devotee.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the principle of 'ananya-gati' (single-minded surrender) as the foundation of spiritual practice. The devotee must remain steadfast regardless of external circumstances or divine tests.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the supreme goal and the only refuge; all material attainments are insignificant.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that Kriya-Yoga aims at realizing the Self as identical with Krishna. Rukmini's rejection of worldly kings mirrors the practitioner's renunciation of material desires.

teachings.teaching: Marriage and household life can be a path to liberation when centered on devotion to God.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's commentary highlights that domestic life, when performed as an offering to Krishna, becomes a form of Kriya-Yoga. The mutual love and service between husband and wife can purify the mind.

teachings.teaching: The body is a temporary vessel; attachment to it leads to suffering.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga practice of disidentification from the body through pranayama and meditation. Rukmini's description of the body as a 'living corpse' underscores the need for inner awakening.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's playful words and actions are meant to deepen the devotee's love and understanding.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma interprets Krishna's teasing as a form of 'lila' that awakens the devotee's inner longing. The apparent rejection is a catalyst for greater surrender.

chapter_summary: Rukmini expresses her deep love and devotion to Krishna, recounting how she chose him over other kings and how he rescued her. Krishna responds with playful and loving words, and the chapter concludes with their intimate conversation and Krishna's domestic life with his queens.

story.title: Rukmini's Loving Lament and Krishna's Playful Response
story.arc: Rukmini expresses her love and jealousy, Krishna reassures her, and they enjoy conjugal bliss.
story.timeline_note: After Krishna's marriage to Rukmini and his other queens, during their domestic life in Dvaraka.
story.comic_potential: Rukmini's playful accusations and Krishna's witty replies create a light-hearted, romantic comedy.

teachings.teaching: The divine couple's intimate conversation illustrates the ideal of loving devotion (bhakti) where the devotee feels a personal relationship with the divine.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this dialogue represents the internal union of the individual self (jiva) with the Supreme Self (Shiva) through the practice of Kriya, where the devotee's longing and the guru's grace meet.

teachings.teaching: Krishna, though the Supreme Lord, plays the role of a householder, demonstrating that divine consciousness can be present in all aspects of life.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes that the householder life is a valid path for spiritual realization when actions are performed with awareness and devotion.

chapter_summary: This chapter lists the sons of Krishna by his chief queens, describes the marriage of Aniruddha to Rocana (daughter of Rukmi), and narrates the dice game between Balarama and Rukmi, which ends with Balarama killing Rukmi after being insulted.

story.title: The Sons of Krishna and the Dice Game with Rukmi
story.arc: Genealogy and conflict resolution
story.timeline_note: After Krishna's marriage to Rukmini and other queens, their sons are born. Later, Aniruddha marries Rocana and Charumati. The dice game occurs during the wedding festivities.
story.comic_potential: The dice game and Balarama's violent reaction to the insult provide dramatic and humorous elements.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's queens and sons are described as expansions of his opulence, yet they cannot fully comprehend his nature.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates that even the closest devotees may not grasp the absolute truth; surrender and practice are needed.

teachings.teaching: The Lord remains unattached despite being surrounded by thousands of wives.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that the yogi should remain detached like Krishna, engaging in worldly activities without being entangled.

teachings.teaching: The queens served Krishna with devotion, performing various services.
teachings.lens_note: This exemplifies the Kriya-Yoga principle of offering all actions to the divine, transforming mundane tasks into spiritual practice.

teachings.teaching: Balarama's anger and killing of Rukmi shows that even great souls may act in anger, but it is for a higher purpose.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma notes that anger can be a tool to uphold dharma when free from personal motive, as Balarama defended his honor.

chapter_summary: King Parīkṣit asks Śuka about the marriage of Aniruddha to Ūṣā, daughter of Bāṇa, and the ensuing battle between Kṛṣṇa and Śiva. Śuka narrates Bāṇa's boon from Śiva, his arrogance, Ūṣā's dream of Aniruddha, Citralekhā's abduction of Aniruddha, and Bāṇa's capture of Aniruddha, leading to the war.

story.title: The Marriage of Aniruddha and Ūṣā
story.arc: Bāṇa's arrogance leads to conflict with Kṛṣṇa; love story of Aniruddha and Ūṣā.
story.timeline_note: After Vāmana's incarnation; during Kṛṣṇa's time in Dvārakā.
story.comic_potential: Ūṣā's dream, Citralekhā's magical abduction, Aniruddha's fight with Bāṇa's army.

teachings.teaching: Devotion to Śiva can grant boons, but arrogance leads to downfall.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, devotion must be coupled with humility; pride in one's powers is a form of ego that obstructs spiritual progress.

teachings.teaching: Divine love transcends physical boundaries; Ūṣā's dream of Aniruddha symbolizes the soul's longing for the divine.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, the soul (jīva) dreams of union with the Supreme (Paramātman); this is the beginning of spiritual awakening.

teachings.teaching: The power of yoga (Citralekhā) can bring the divine to the devotee.
teachings.lens_note: Yogic powers (siddhis) are tools for divine service, not for personal gain; Citralekhā uses her yoga to unite Ūṣā with Aniruddha.

teachings.teaching: Even a demon's daughter can be a devotee; Ūṣā's love for Aniruddha shows that divine grace is available to all.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, the heart's purity matters more than birth; Ūṣā's sincere love purifies her.

chapter_summary: Lord Krishna and Balarama lead the Vrishnis to rescue Aniruddha from Bana's fortress. A fierce battle ensues between Krishna and Shiva, and between their respective armies. Krishna defeats Shiva's forces, cuts off Bana's arms, and spares him due to Brahma's boon to Prahlada. Bana surrenders and returns Aniruddha and Usha.

story.title: The Rescue of Aniruddha
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Aniruddha's abduction by Usha; four months pass before the Vrishnis learn of his captivity.
story.comic_potential: High: cosmic battle with gods, demons, and magical fevers; Bana's thousand arms being cut off; Kotara's naked plea; Jvara's surrender.

teachings.teaching: The supreme Lord is the sole reality, and all phenomena are manifestations of His maya.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: This echoes the teaching that the practitioner must realize the Self as the witness (sakshi) behind all modifications of mind and matter.

teachings.teaching: One who neglects the Lord's feet in favor of sense objects is deluded and suffers.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: This aligns with the emphasis on turning the senses inward and fixing the mind on the inner Self through pranayama and meditation.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's grace is available to all who surrender, even demons like Bana.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: Surrender (prapatti) is a key step; through Kriya-Yoga, the practitioner offers all actions to the divine, leading to liberation.

chapter_summary: After being blessed by Lord Shiva, Lord Krishna returns to his capital city Dvaraka with his wives, entering amidst grand decorations and celebratory sounds. The chapter concludes with a verse stating that one who recites this account of Krishna's victory over Shiva in the morning will never face defeat.

story.title: Krishna's victorious return to Dvaraka
story.arc: Krishna's return after the battle with Shiva
story.timeline_note: After the battle with Shiva, Krishna returns to Dvaraka.
story.comic_potential: A grand procession with decorated streets, flags, and musical instruments, with Krishna entering triumphantly.

teachings.teaching: Reciting the account of Krishna's victory over Shiva in the morning ensures one will never face defeat.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the power of remembrance (smriti) as a practice to align with divine will and overcome obstacles.

chapter_summary: King Nṛga, a former king of the Ikṣvāku dynasty, recounts his fall from grace due to an inadvertent misdeed involving a Brahmin's cow. After unknowingly giving away a lost cow that belonged to one Brahmin to another, both Brahmins refused his compensation, leading to his death and subsequent rebirth as a lizard in a well. Upon being rescued by Kṛṣṇa, Nṛga regains his divine form and ascends to heaven. Kṛṣṇa then teaches the Yadus about the severe consequences of misappropriating Brahmin property.

story.title: The Deliverance of King Nṛga
story.arc: Kṛṣṇa's pastimes in Dvārakā
story.timeline_note: Occurs during Kṛṣṇa's residence in Dvārakā, after the defeat of Bāṇāsura (Chapter 63).
story.comic_potential: High: A king trapped as a lizard in a well, rescued by Kṛṣṇa, then ascending to heaven in a celestial chariot, followed by a moral lesson on Brahmin property.

teachings.teaching: Misappropriating a Brahmin's property, even unknowingly, leads to severe karmic consequences, including rebirth in hellish conditions.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this underscores the principle of karma and the importance of mindfulness in action (kriya). Every action, even unintentional, has consequences that must be purified through awareness and surrender to the divine.

teachings.teaching: One should never harm a Brahmin, even if they are at fault; instead, bow to them always.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes humility and non-violence as essential practices. Bowing to Brahmins symbolizes surrendering the ego and recognizing the divine in all beings.

teachings.teaching: The sin of stealing Brahmin property is worse than poison, as it destroys the entire family for generations.
teachings.lens_note: This teaching highlights the gravity of actions that disrupt the social and spiritual order. In Kriya-Yoga, such warnings serve to cultivate discernment (viveka) and adherence to dharma.

chapter_summary: Balarama visits Nanda's Gokula, is warmly received by the gopas and gopis. The gopis express their longing for Krishna and their sorrow at his absence. Balarama consoles them with Krishna's messages. He stays for two months, enjoying the Yamuna forest with the gopis. The goddess Varuni appears, and Balarama drinks. In a drunken state, he calls the Yamuna to come for water sports; when she disobeys, he drags her with his plow. She begs forgiveness, and he releases her. He then sports in the water and dresses splendidly. The chapter ends noting that Balarama's pastimes with the gopis continue.

story.title: Balarama's Visit to Vraja and the Yamuna Incident
story.arc: Balarama's return to Gokula, his reunion with the gopas and gopis, his two-month stay, the appearance of Varuni, his drunken command to the Yamuna, and the river's submission.
story.timeline_note: After Krishna and Balarama killed Kamsa and moved to Mathura, Balarama visits Gokula. The events occur during the months of Madhu and Madhava (spring).
story.comic_potential: Balarama's drunken state, his plowing of the Yamuna, and the river's personified fear and submission offer vivid visual comedy.

teachings.teaching: The gopis' intense love for Krishna, expressed through their lament and questions, illustrates the soul's longing for the divine.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, the gopis' yearning represents the practitioner's deep desire for union with the inner Self, and their tears are the purification of emotions through devotion.

teachings.teaching: Balarama's anger at the Yamuna and subsequent forgiveness shows that even divine beings experience emotions, but ultimately compassion prevails.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework interprets this as the control of prana (life force) through discipline; Balarama's plow symbolizes the channeling of energy, and the river's submission represents the mind's surrender to higher will.

teachings.teaching: The gopis' statement that they abandoned their families for Krishna, yet he left them, teaches non-attachment and the testing of devotion.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this is the stage where the practitioner must let go of worldly ties and endure the apparent absence of the divine, trusting in the inner process.

chapter_summary: Paundraka, the king of Karusha, deluded by children's praise, claims to be Vasudeva and sends a messenger to Krishna demanding surrender. Krishna laughs, warns him, then kills Paundraka and his ally the king of Kashi in battle. The king of Kashi's son Sudakshina performs a ritual to invoke a fiery demon, which Krishna destroys with his Sudarshana chakra, which then burns Varanasi. The chapter ends with the glory of hearing this story.

story.title: The Killing of Paundraka and the King of Kashi
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes as the supreme lord, demonstrating his supremacy over false claimants and protecting his devotees.
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Balarama's visit to Nanda's Vraja, during Krishna's residence in Dvaraka.
story.comic_potential: High: Paundraka's deluded imitation of Krishna, the humorous reaction of the assembly, the dramatic battle, and the fiery demon provide vivid visual and comedic elements.

teachings.teaching: False pride and imitation of the divine lead to destruction.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the danger of ego (ahamkara) masquerading as spiritual attainment. True realization requires surrender, not imitation.

teachings.teaching: The Lord protects his devotees and destroys the wicked.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that divine grace (kripa) is the active force; the Lord's intervention is a metaphor for the inner purification that burns away impurities.

teachings.teaching: Hearing the Lord's glories purifies one from sin.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the practice of shravana (hearing) as a key Kriya-Yoga technique for inner transformation.

chapter_summary: King Parīkṣit asks Śuka about Balarāma's extraordinary deeds. Śuka narrates how the monkey Dvivida, friend of Naraka, wreaks havoc across the land in revenge for Naraka's death. He attacks hermitages, pollutes sacred fires, and imprisons people. Hearing Balarāma's sweet singing on Raivataka mountain, Dvivida goes there and insults Balarāma and his women. A fierce battle ensues: Dvivida hurls trees and rocks, but Balarāma breaks them with his plow and mace. Finally, Balarāma kills Dvivida with a blow to the chest. The gods praise him, and he returns to Dvārakā.

story.title: The Slaying of Dvivida
story.arc: Balarāma's pastimes
story.timeline_note: After Naraka's death, Dvivida seeks revenge.
story.comic_potential: Dvivida's antics, including showing his rear end to the women, and Balarāma's drunken singing provide comic elements.

teachings.teaching: The divine protects dharma by destroying those who disrupt cosmic order.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the principle of 'kriyā' as dynamic action that restores balance; Balarāma's effortless destruction of Dvivida symbolizes the yogi's ability to dissolve obstacles through disciplined practice.

teachings.teaching: Even a powerful demon cannot withstand the Lord's might.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's commentary emphasizes that the 'muṣala' (mace) represents the concentrated will of the yogi, which shatters all impurities.

chapter_summary: Samba, son of Krishna and Jambavati, abducts Lakshmana, daughter of Duryodhana, from her svayamvara. The Kauravas capture him. Balarama goes to Hastinapura to negotiate, but the Kauravas insult him. Enraged, Balarama threatens to destroy the city. The Kauravas surrender and offer gifts. Balarama forgives them.

story.title: The Abduction of Lakshmana and Balarama's Intervention
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: Occurs after the events of the Mahabharata war? Actually before the war, as Duryodhana is alive and ruling.
story.comic_potential: High: Samba's bold abduction, the Kauravas' arrogance, Balarama's wrath and the city nearly being dragged into the Ganga.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's anger is for the sake of instructing the world, not out of hatred or envy.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates that divine actions, even wrathful, are pedagogical, guiding beings toward dharma.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the sole cause of creation, maintenance, and dissolution, and is independent.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework emphasizes the Lord's absolute sovereignty; all actions are his play.

teachings.teaching: Humility and surrender are the proper responses to the Lord's power.
teachings.lens_note: The Kauravas' surrender exemplifies the Kriya-Yoga principle of self-surrender (prapatti) as a means to grace.

chapter_summary: After accepting the gifts, Lord Balarāma, accompanied by his son and daughter-in-law, departed, blessed by friends. He entered his city and, meeting his affectionate relatives, narrated all his deeds among the Yadu chiefs in the assembly. Even today, the city shows a raised southern bank along the Ganges, indicating Rāma's prowess.

story.title: Balarāma's Return and Narration
story.arc: Balarāma returns to his city and recounts his adventures to the Yadu assembly.
story.timeline_note: After the events of the previous chapter where Balarāma received gifts.
story.comic_potential: Balarāma's dramatic narration and the city's lasting mark of his prowess.

teachings.teaching: One should share one's experiences with close associates and family.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, sharing experiences with like-minded practitioners strengthens the collective sadhana and reinforces the lessons learned.

chapter_summary: Narada visits Dvaraka to see how Krishna simultaneously lives with his 16,000 wives. He witnesses Krishna in each palace engaged in different activities, demonstrating his yoga-maya. Narada praises Krishna and departs, realizing the Lord's simultaneous presence everywhere.

story.title: Narada's Visit to Dvaraka: The Yoga-Maya of Krishna
story.arc: Narada, curious about Krishna's marriage to 16,000 wives, goes to Dvaraka. He sees Krishna in one palace with a wife, then in another playing dice, then with children, bathing, performing sacrifices, etc. Finally, Narada understands Krishna's simultaneous presence everywhere and leaves glorifying him.
story.timeline_note: After Naraka's death and the marriage of Krishna to many wives.
story.comic_potential: Narada's bewildered expressions as he moves from one palace to another, each time seeing Krishna in a different activity, could be played for humor.

teachings.teaching: Krishna's simultaneous presence in multiple forms demonstrates his yoga-maya, the divine power to be everywhere at once.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the principle of 'eka-vapu' (one body) manifesting as many, akin to the yogi's ability to be in multiple states of consciousness simultaneously.

teachings.teaching: Even though Krishna is the Supreme Lord, he follows human dharma to set an example.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that the yogi should not abandon worldly duties but perform them with detachment, as Krishna does.

teachings.teaching: Narada's realization that Krishna's pastimes are meant to purify the world and inspire devotion.
teachings.lens_note: The practice of singing and hearing the Lord's lila is a key Kriya-Yoga technique for purification.

chapter_summary: Krishna's daily routine in Dwaraka, including his morning meditation, worship, and court activities. A messenger from imprisoned kings arrives seeking help against Jarāsandha. Nārada appears and informs Krishna about Yudhiṣṭhira's plan to perform the Rājasūya sacrifice. Krishna consults Uddhava.

story.title: Krishna's Daily Routine and the Imprisoned Kings
story.arc: Preparation for the Rājasūya sacrifice
story.timeline_note: Morning in Dwaraka, after Krishna's return from battle with Jarāsandha.
story.comic_potential: Krishna's elaborate morning routine contrasted with the sudden appearance of a distressed messenger and the dramatic entrance of Nārada.

teachings.teaching: Krishna meditates on the Supreme Brahman, the self-luminous, immutable reality beyond the three modes.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this represents the practitioner's absorption in the inner light (ātma-jyoti) through pratyāhāra and dhāraṇā.

teachings.teaching: The six waves (ṣaḍ-ūrmi) of material existence—hunger, thirst, grief, delusion, old age, and death—do not affect those who enter Sudharmā.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma interprets Sudharmā as the state of samādhi where the yogi transcends bodily limitations.

teachings.teaching: The kings lament that worldly happiness is dream-like and dependent on others; they seek Krishna's shelter.
teachings.lens_note: This echoes the Kriya-Yoga teaching that sense pleasures are fleeting and the true bliss is within, attained through inner stillness.

teachings.teaching: Nārada praises Krishna's pastimes as liberating the conditioned soul from saṃsāra.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that hearing and chanting Krishna's līlā is a form of bhakti-yoga that purifies the mind.

teachings.teaching: Even the lowest (ante-vasāyina) are purified by hearing, chanting, and meditating on Krishna.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Sharma's view that Kriya-Yoga is accessible to all, regardless of background.

chapter_summary: Uddhava advises Krishna to kill Jarāsandha to enable the Rājasūya sacrifice, and Krishna agrees. He travels to Indraprastha, where Yudhiṣṭhira and the Pāṇḍavas welcome him. Krishna stays for several months, enjoying pastimes with Arjuna.

story.title: Uddhava's Advice and Krishna's Journey to Indraprastha
story.arc: Preparation for Rājasūya
story.timeline_note: After Krishna's return from Mithilā, before the Rājasūya sacrifice.
story.comic_potential: Krishna's grand procession to Indraprastha, the emotional reunion with the Pāṇḍavas, and the humorous scene of women rushing to see Krishna.

teachings.teaching: The killing of Jarāsandha is necessary for the Rājasūya sacrifice and for the welfare of the world.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this represents the removal of obstacles (kleshas) through determined action (kriya) to achieve a higher purpose (yajña).

teachings.teaching: One should not refuse a request from brāhmaṇas.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga principle of humility and service to the wise, which purifies the mind.

teachings.teaching: Krishna is the ultimate controller of creation and destruction, manifesting as Brahmā and Śiva.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this points to the realization of the Self as the witness (sākṣī) of all phenomena.

chapter_summary: Yudhiṣṭhira proposes to perform the Rājasūya sacrifice. Kṛṣṇa advises him to first conquer all kings. Yudhiṣṭhira sends his brothers to conquer the directions. They bring tribute. Jarāsandha, the powerful king of Magadha, remains unconquered. Kṛṣṇa, Bhīma, and Arjuna, disguised as brāhmaṇas, go to Girivraja. They challenge Jarāsandha to a duel. Bhīma fights Jarāsandha and, with Kṛṣṇa's hint, splits his body in two. Kṛṣṇa installs Jarāsandha's son Sahadeva as king and releases the imprisoned kings.

story.title: The Slaying of Jarāsandha and the Preparations for the Rājasūya
story.arc: Preparation for the Rājasūya sacrifice; conquest of the world; defeat of Jarāsandha.
story.timeline_note: After the Pāṇḍavas' exile and return, before the great war of Kurukṣetra.
story.comic_potential: High: Disguised heroes, a boastful villain, a dramatic duel with a clever hint, and a gory but victorious split.

teachings.teaching: The Rājasūya sacrifice is the best of sacrifices and pleases all beings.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, the Rājasūya symbolizes the culmination of self-discipline and service, where the king (representing the individual self) offers the fruits of all actions to the Supreme.

teachings.teaching: One should conquer the mind and senses before undertaking great actions.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes inner conquest as prerequisite for outer success. Kṛṣṇa's advice to conquer all kings mirrors the yogic conquest of the senses.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is conquered by one who has conquered his own self.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, self-mastery leads to divine grace. Kṛṣṇa says he is conquered by the self-controlled, reflecting the principle that inner purity attracts the divine.

teachings.teaching: Even an unstable body can be used to attain eternal fame.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga teaches that the body is a temporary instrument for spiritual practice. Using it for dharma yields lasting results.

teachings.teaching: Examples of those who attained the eternal through the temporary: Hariścandra, Rantideva, etc.
teachings.lens_note: These exemplars show that sincere practice (kriya) and sacrifice lead to liberation, a core tenet of Kriya-Yoga.

teachings.teaching: A true kṣatriya should give even his life for a brāhmaṇa's request.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, the brāhmaṇa represents the spiritual seeker; serving such a one is a high duty.

chapter_summary: Krishna releases 20,800 kings imprisoned by Jarasandha, who praise Krishna and renounce worldly desires. Krishna instructs them on devotion and dharma, then honors them and sends them back to their kingdoms. He then returns to Indraprastha with Bhima and Arjuna, where Yudhishthira rejoices.

story.title: Release of the Imprisoned Kings
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Dvaraka and beyond
story.timeline_note: After Jarasandha's death, before the Rajasuya sacrifice.
story.comic_potential: The dramatic release of 20,800 kings from a mountain cave, their awe at seeing Krishna, and the subsequent return to Indraprastha with triumphant conch-blowing.

teachings.teaching: Wealth and power lead to pride and downfall; detachment from material opulence is essential.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: This echoes the principle of vairagya (dispassion) as a prerequisite for inner purification. The kings' realization that material prosperity is transient aligns with the yogic practice of detaching from sensory objects.

teachings.teaching: Devotion to Krishna (bhakti) is the ultimate refuge and the means to transcend samsara.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: Bhakti is the culmination of Kriya-Yoga, where the practitioner's entire being is offered to the divine. The kings' prayer for constant remembrance of Krishna's lotus feet exemplifies this surrender.

teachings.teaching: One should perform righteous actions (dharma) without attachment, dedicating them to Krishna.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: This is karma-yoga in action—performing duties as an offering, which purifies the mind and leads to liberation. Krishna's instruction to rule with dharma while keeping the mind on him is a direct application.

chapter_summary: Yudhishthira, having heard of Krishna's slaying of Jarasandha, praises Krishna as the Supreme Lord. He then performs the Rajasuya sacrifice with many sages and kings present. During the ceremony, Sahadeva declares Krishna the most worthy of honor, which enrages Shishupala, who insults Krishna. Krishna kills Shishupala with his discus, and Shishupala's soul merges into Krishna. The sacrifice concludes successfully.

story.title: The Rajasuya Sacrifice and the Slaying of Shishupala
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes as the Supreme Lord
story.timeline_note: After the killing of Jarasandha, Yudhishthira performs the Rajasuya. Shishupala's death occurs during the ceremony.
story.comic_potential: The dramatic confrontation between Shishupala and Krishna, with Shishupala's insults and Krishna's calm response, followed by the sudden decapitation, has high comic potential.

teachings.teaching: Krishna is the Supreme Lord, non-dual, and the source of all. Worshiping Him is equivalent to worshiping all beings.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this teaching emphasizes that all actions and practices should be directed towards the inner Self (Krishna), unifying all dualities.

teachings.teaching: One who hears blasphemy of the Lord and does not leave is fallen.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Kriya-Yoga's emphasis on satsanga (association with truth) and avoiding negative influences that disturb the mind.

teachings.teaching: Constant enmity and meditation on the Lord, even in hatred, can lead to liberation, as seen in Shishupala's merging into Krishna.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga interprets this as the power of focused attention (dhyana) on the Divine, regardless of the emotional tone, eventually leading to union.

chapter_summary: The chapter describes the conclusion of the Rajasuya sacrifice performed by Yudhishthira, where all beings honor Krishna and the sacrifice. Only Duryodhana, envious of the Pandavas' prosperity, remains displeased. The chapter ends with a verse glorifying the recitation of Krishna's deeds, including the killing of Shishupala and the liberation of the king.

story.title: Conclusion of the Rajasuya Sacrifice
story.arc: End of the Rajasuya episode; Duryodhana's envy sets stage for future conflict.
story.timeline_note: After the Rajasuya sacrifice, before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: Duryodhana's brooding envy contrasted with the joyous departure of others.

teachings.teaching: Recitation of Krishna's deeds (like killing Shishupala) liberates from all sins.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the power of kirtana (chanting) as a purifying practice that aligns the practitioner with divine will.

chapter_summary: This chapter describes the Rājasūya sacrifice of King Yudhiṣṭhira, the grand festivities, the distribution of duties among the Pāṇḍavas and Kauravas, the avabhṛtha ceremony, and the humiliation of Duryodhana due to his confusion in the magical hall, which fuels his envy and anger.

story.title: The Rājasūya Sacrifice and Duryodhana's Humiliation
story.arc: Yudhiṣṭhira's Rājasūya and the seeds of the Kurukṣetra war
story.timeline_note: After the Rājasūya sacrifice, before the dice game and exile.
story.comic_potential: Duryodhana's confusion between water and solid ground in the magical hall, leading to his fall and laughter from the women and kings.

teachings.teaching: The Rājasūya sacrifice demonstrates the ideal of dharma and the importance of service and humility even for kings.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the principle of karma-yoga: performing one's duties with detachment and as an offering to the divine. The detailed distribution of tasks shows how every action can be a form of worship.

teachings.teaching: Pride and envy lead to downfall, as seen in Duryodhana's humiliation.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework emphasizes the purification of the mind (citta-śuddhi). Duryodhana's envy and pride are obstacles to spiritual progress, and his humiliation serves as a lesson in ego-dissolution.

teachings.teaching: The avabhṛtha ceremony symbolizes purification and the removal of sins.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the cleansing of subtle impurities through disciplined practice and surrender to the divine. The bath in the Ganges represents the final purification after the sacrifice.

chapter_summary: Shalva, a friend of Shishupala, vows to destroy the Yadavas after being humiliated at Rukmini's wedding. He propitiates Lord Shiva and obtains a flying city (Saubha) from the demon Maya. He attacks Dvaraka, causing devastation. Pradyumna leads the Yadava defense, countering Shalva's illusions. During battle, Pradyumna is struck down by Shalva's minister Dyuman, but his charioteer saves him. Pradyumna rebukes the charioteer for retreating, but the charioteer explains he acted out of duty to protect him.

story.title: The Slaying of Shalva and the Destruction of Saubha
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes: subduing enemies of the Yadavas
story.timeline_note: After Shishupala's death and Rukmini's wedding; before Krishna's later battles.
story.comic_potential: High: flying city, illusions, dramatic battle, Pradyumna's rage at being rescued.

teachings.teaching: Even a powerful king like Shalva, deluded by pride, makes a rash vow and undergoes severe penance to obtain a boon, but ultimately faces defeat.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates how ego-driven actions (kama) lead to bondage; Shalva's vow stems from wounded pride, not dharma.

teachings.teaching: Pradyumna's anger at being rescued shows the warrior's code of honor; the charioteer's response emphasizes dharma over personal pride.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would highlight the charioteer's action as an example of 'kriya' (right action) guided by duty, not emotion. Pradyumna's initial reaction reflects the ego's attachment to reputation.

chapter_summary: Krishna battles King Shalva, who uses illusion to appear to kill Vasudeva. Krishna overcomes grief, destroys Shalva's flying city Saubha, and beheads Shalva. Dantavakra then approaches in anger.

story.title: The Slaying of Shalva
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes as a warrior king
story.timeline_note: After the Rajasuya sacrifice and Shishupala's death, Krishna returns to Dvaraka to find Shalva attacking. The battle lasts 27 days.
story.comic_potential: High: flying city, illusion of father's death, dramatic beheading with a discus.

teachings.teaching: Even the Supreme Lord can appear to be affected by grief and illusion due to his human-like pastimes, but he is always beyond such limitations.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: This shows that the yogi may experience temporary emotional disturbances but should recognize them as māyā and return to self-awareness.

teachings.teaching: One should not boast but demonstrate prowess through action.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: Action (kriya) is superior to mere words; the yogi should practice rather than proclaim.

chapter_summary: Krishna kills Dantavakra and his brother Viduratha, then returns to Dvaraka. Balarama, avoiding the Kuru-Pandava war, goes on a pilgrimage, visits Naimisha forest, and kills the demon Balvala who was disturbing a sacrifice. The sages prescribe a penance for Balarama.

story.title: The Killing of Dantavakra and Balvala
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes and Balarama's pilgrimage
story.timeline_note: After the killing of Shishupala, Shalva, and Paundraka; before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: High: dramatic duels, divine weapons, pilgrimage montage, demonic disturbance of sacrifice.

teachings.teaching: Krishna as Yogeshvara is unaffected by apparent defeat; he is the supreme controller.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the state of the yogi who remains undisturbed by external events, rooted in the self.

teachings.teaching: One who is learned but undisciplined and arrogant gains no benefit from knowledge.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that true knowledge must be integrated through practice (kriya); mere intellectual accumulation is fruitless.

teachings.teaching: The Lord incarnates to destroy the hypocrites who wear the garb of dharma.
